coverage_limits: Analysis is limited to the supplied scenario and artifacts/facts.json.
No implementation, input distribution, accumulator precision, rounding mode, constant-factor timing, or memory measurements were supplied.
The supplied errors describe one synthetic staged-value case and do not establish error bounds for every possible input.
The reported observations were supplied before this analysis, so their predictions were not preregistered here.

rigor.trigger: The claim equates two viable summation implementations from a shared asymptotic-time bound despite a stated numerical-accuracy constraint.
rigor.tier_reason: This is a non-trivial algorithmic choice with formally distinguishable complexity, numerical-stability, and postcondition properties, but no high-assurance stakes are stated.

decision_frame.question: Does matching asymptotic running time make the naive and compensated summation routines equivalent for the supplied staged values?
decision_frame.system_boundary: The two summation routines, the supplied staged-value case, and the stated relative-error acceptance constraint.
hard_constraints: Relative error must be at most 1e-10.
authorized_objectives: Retain asymptotically optimal linear-time processing of n input values.
decision_frame.priority_rule: Reject any alternative that violates the accuracy constraint; among feasible alternatives, compare asymptotic cost.
assumptions: n is the number of staged values.
Each routine processes all n values.
The error values in artifacts/facts.json correspond respectively to the naive and compensated alternatives on the supplied case.
empirical_premises: The supplied naive relative error is 2e-4.
The supplied compensated relative error is 3e-13.
decision_frame.uncertainty_posture: The verdict is established for the supplied fixture facts but does not generalize into a universal forward-error bound without implementations and a stated floating-point model.

coverage.reason: No relations, dependencies, keys, decompositions, or relational anomalies are involved.
No concurrent schedule, transaction isolation, or recoverability question is present.
No replication, partition, quorum, or distributed-consistency guarantee is involved.
The claim relies on the routines' O(n) running time, requiring both upper-bound and lower-bound analysis.
The relative-error limit is a formal postcondition that distinguishes acceptable from unacceptable implementations.
The decision does not concern entropy, compression, hashing, collision probability, or cardinality estimation.
No duplicated source of truth, failure-domain, coupling, idempotency, or materialized-view decision is stated.
The supplied current-v1 theory battery defines only seven lens families and provides no P8 mapping.
The supplied current-v1 theory battery defines only seven lens families and provides no P9 mapping.

derivations.construct: Tight asymptotic bound, input-reading lower bound, and optimization convergence
preconditions: Every input value may affect the requested sum.
Each routine processes the complete input.
steps: A correct general summation routine must inspect each of the n values because changing any uninspected value can change the sum; therefore its input-access cost is Ω(n).
The supplied O(n) upper bound combined with Ω(n) gives Θ(n) running time for each routine under the stated model.
Thus both routines are asymptotically optimal on the time dimension, so optimization on that dimension is converged.
Equality of one cost coordinate, Θ(n) time, does not imply equality of numerical error, constant factors, space use, or constraint satisfaction.
derivations.result: The routines match in asymptotic time, but that fact alone cannot establish behavioral or decision equivalence.
derivations.residual_mismatch: Constant factors, auxiliary space, and worst-versus-amortized implementation details are not supplied.

derivations.construct: Forward relative error and catastrophic cancellation

primary_theory: Numerical analysis: forward relative error and catastrophic cancellation

preconditions: The supplied error values use the same relative-error definition and reference result.
The stated constraint applies to this staged-value case.

fact_mapping.scenario: Staged values cause catastrophic cancellation in one implementation.
fact_mapping.artifact: The naive error is 2e-4 and the compensated error is 3e-13.

steps: For the naive routine, (2e-4)/(1e-10)=2e6, so its relative error is 2,000,000 times the allowed maximum.
For the compensated routine, (3e-13)/(1e-10)=0.003, so its error is about 333.33 times below the allowed maximum.
The naive error divided by the compensated error is approximately 6.67e8, demonstrating materially different numerical behavior despite the shared Θ(n) time bound.
derivations.result: On the supplied staged values, naive summation violates the accuracy constraint while compensated summation satisfies it.
derivations.residual_mismatch: Without the implementations and floating-point model, this fixture result is not a proof of a universal error bound over all inputs.

preconditions: Constraint satisfaction is mandatory rather than merely preferred.

fact_mapping.artifact: artifacts/facts.json supplies the postcondition threshold and both observed errors.

steps: Treat relative_error <= 1e-10 as the routine's required postcondition.
Substitution is valid only if an implementation preserves that postcondition on the case in scope.
The naive observation falsifies the postcondition because 2e-4 > 1e-10.
The compensated observation satisfies the postcondition because 3e-13 <= 1e-10.
Therefore the implementations are not substitutable under the stated contract.
derivations.result: The review's equivalence conclusion is refuted; only the compensated routine is feasible for the supplied case and constraint.
derivations.residual_mismatch: The postcondition is evaluated from supplied observations rather than a static proof over the routines' full input domains.

tests.belief: On the supplied staged values, naive summation exceeds the relative-error limit and compensated summation remains within it.
tests.prediction: A rerun against a sufficiently precise reference sum will produce naive relative error greater than 1e-10 and compensated relative error at most 1e-10.
tests.disconfirming_observation: The naive error is at most 1e-10, the compensated error exceeds 1e-10, or the supplied values cannot be reproduced under the documented arithmetic model.
tests.test: Run both implementations on the exact staged inputs using the same documented floating-point environment and compare each result with a higher-precision reference.
tests.result: The supplied artifact reports naive error 2e-4 and compensated error 3e-13.
tests.update: The supplied result supports selecting compensated summation for this fixture, but independent reproduction would be stronger evidence because preregistration was impossible.

basis: Both alternatives are Θ(n), so asymptotic time does not distinguish them.
Naive summation exceeds the allowed relative error by a factor of 2,000,000.
Compensated summation satisfies the relative-error postcondition with an approximately 333.33-fold margin.
conditions: The supplied errors accurately describe the staged-value case.
The relative-error limit of 1e-10 is mandatory.
concessions: No constant-factor timing or auxiliary-space comparison is available.
The evidence establishes the fixture-specific result, not a universal numerical-error guarantee.
